_doc.purpose: Layer-B semantic oracle — retrieval (live) + conflict (shape-only, filled by the Conflict-sensor vision's section 6.3). SEPARATE from the frozen deterministic oracle.reference.json. Assertions here are BANDED, not exact: embedding order drifts by design.

retrieval_entry.scope: null on the raw scoring path (vector_store.query is scope-blind by contract); consulted only by the MCP-surface smoke
retrieval_entry.expect_relevant: slugs that SHOULD surface in the top-k
retrieval_entry.expect_absent: hard negatives — plausible-but-wrong slugs that a healthy ranking keeps OUT of the top-k (precision guard)
retrieval_entry.measure_only: when true, no recall floor is asserted — the fixture exists to MEASURE a known-hard case (e.g. the cross-domain embedding-recall ceiling), reported but never gating
retrieval_entry.min_recall_at_k: OMITTED until calibrated from a first measured run (never pre-pinned). Added deliberately, reviewed like --update-golden. Absent → the live test applies only a loose smoke floor; the real measurement lives in the metrics report + baseline diff.

conflict_entry._status: SHAPE ONLY — fixtures authored by the Conflict-sensor vision (v0.2 V1) section 6.3, not here. Do NOT call any LLM judgment path from Layer B yet.
conflict_entry.proposal: slug of the incoming decision being judged
conflict_entry.candidate: slug of the existing decision it is judged against
conflict_entry.kind: one of: genuine-contradiction | same-polarity-agreement | cross-domain-structural | global-vs-scoped-narrows
conflict_entry.expected_tenable: bool — true means the pair coexists fine (must NOT be flagged); false means a real contradiction the sensor SHOULD flag not-tenable
conflict_entry.expected_confidence_band: [low, high] band on the judge's confidence, for the calibration curve

retrieval.query: How do we resolve two people editing the same file at the same time?
retrieval.notes: Both concurrent-edit strategies are the precedent; blob storage and rate-limiting are topical noise.

retrieval.query: What format is Harbor's configuration file written in and how is it validated?
retrieval.notes: Same-polarity near-duplicate cluster (the #34 case) — for retrieval they co-cluster. Hard negative harbor-structured-logging is plausibly-near-but-decisively-wrong: it is saturated with format/JSON vocabulary (the direct rival to YAML) so drift pulls it in first, yet log-output format is unambiguously NOT config-file format — the earliest-firing tripwire at the true decision boundary. (harbor-storage-is-sqlite was the original choice but is a corpus HUB — it cracks top-5 of 5/6 fixtures regardless of query, so it measures hubness not discrimination; demoted to the measure_only twin below.)

retrieval.query: What format is Harbor's configuration file written in and how is it validated?
retrieval.notes: measure_only twin of the config fixture — tracks how close the (infra-adjacent, hub-like) storage decision sits to a config-format query. NOT gated (it fires on hubness, not this query's ranking quality); recorded because the config/storage semantic entanglement is a useful datapoint for later conflict-detection threshold calibration.

retrieval.query: What happens to a file when a user deletes it — is it recoverable?
retrieval.notes: The undeclared genuine-contradiction pair. Retrieval MUST co-surface both, or section 6.3's conflict sensor can never judge a contradiction it cannot retrieve.

retrieval.query: Kur Harbor saugo Lietuvos naudotojų duomenis?
retrieval.notes: P9 multilingual recall — a Lithuanian query must retrieve the Lithuanian data-residency cluster. If it cannot, the sensor is blind to non-English contradictions.

retrieval.query: Which Harbor API endpoints require authentication and which are public?
retrieval.notes: Global rule + its scoped narrowing exception; a data point on whether a global and its scoped exception co-rank.

retrieval.query: Should Harbor components share global singletons or pass dependencies in explicitly?
retrieval.notes: Cross-domain structural pair (architecture vs performance), opposed in substance. Probe A DELIBERATELY shares vocabulary ('singleton'/'global') with both decisions — it measures recall WHEN a lexical bridge exists. Paired with probe B (no shared vocabulary) to isolate lexical vs semantic recall: if A co-ranks but B does not, the recall was lexical, characterising the embedding-recall ceiling the spec names. measure_only.

retrieval.query: How should Harbor manage state that is shared between concurrent requests?
retrieval.notes: Probe B — the same cross-domain pair, but the query shares NO gift words ('singleton'/'global'/'dependency') with either decision. Tests the TRUE semantic ceiling: do an architecture decision and a performance decision co-surface on meaning alone? Contrast with probe A is the measurement. measure_only.
